coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorometric probe for doxycycline in food by using bovine serum albumin protected Au nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yinyin
Wang, Xini
Lu, Changfang
Wu, Wei
Wang, Xianxiang
coredata.dc:description: Doxycycline (DOX) is widely used as feed additive and may remain in animal food. Thus, the detection of DOX in food field has become important. This work has reported a simple and rapid strategy to detect DOX by ratiometric fluorescence method without any sensitization materials based on bovine serum albumin (BSA) protected Au nanoclusters (BSA-Au NCs). Under excitation at 390 nm, the BSA-Au NCs has displayed two distinct emission peaks at 510 nm and 640 nm which are respectively originated from BSA and Au NCs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I 510/I 640 of BSA-Au NCs probe has presented a good linear range (0.1–30 μM) and 0.006 μM limit of detection for DOX. This method was used successfully to detect DOX in food samples and the good recoveries reached 96.7–103.7%. The research has showed that potential application of BSA-Au NCs in food field because of its convenient synthesis scheme and excellent selectivity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Fluorescence
Doxycycline
BSA-Au NCs
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of BSA-Au NCs
Detection mechanism of the BSA-Au NCs for DOX
Selective and sensitive determination of DOX
Determination of DOX in food sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of the BSA-Au NCs and schematic diagram for DOX determination.
(a) HRTEM images of BSA-Au NCs and size range distribution of BSA-Au NCs; (b) FT-IR of the BSA-Au NCs; (c) XPS spectra of BSA-Au NCs and (d) Au 4f XPS spectra of the BSA-Au NCs.

(a) UV–vis, fluorescence spectra of the BSA-Au NCs and its color under sunlight, ultraviolet light; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-Au NCs excited from 310 nm to 410 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) The influence of different pH on the BSA-Au NCs/DOX (20 μM) detection system and (b) Fluorescence intensities of BSA-Au NCs/DOX (20 μM) detection system at different time after mixing.

(a) Fluorescence color under 365 nm UV lamp and from left to right: DOX (pH = 12), DOX (pH = 6.65), BSA (pH = 12), BSA (pH = 6.65), BSA-Au NCs (pH = 12), BSA-Au NCs (pH = 6.65), the mixture of DOX and BSA (pH = 12), the mixture of DOX and BSA (pH = 6.65), the mixture of DOX and BSA-Au NCs (pH = 12), the mixture of DOX and BSA-Au NCs (pH = 6.65). The concentration of DOX was 200 μM; (b) The ultraviolet mechanism of BSA (pH = 6.5), BSA (pH = 12), BSA-Au NCs and DOX (20 μM); (c) The fluorescence spectra of BSA (pH = 6.5), BSA (pH = 12), BSA-Au NCs and DOX (10 μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Selectivity analysis for DOX detection. The concentrations of DOX and other target molecules were 15 μM. (b) Competitive analysis for DOX (15 μM) detection in the presence of other interfering substances (15 μM). (1) Blank, (2) OTC, (3) TC, (4) CTC, (5) CB, (6) AMK, (7) AZM, (8) PEF, (9) TIL, (10) MBF, (11) APR, (12) ENR, (13) STR, (14) CIP, (15) Ca2+, (16) K+, (17) Mg2+, (18) Fe3+, (19) Glutamine, (20) Arginine, (21) Glutamate, (22) Methionine, (23) Serine, (24) Asparagine, (25) Cysteine, (26) Lysine, (27) Histidine, (28) Glutathione, (29) α-chymotrypsin, (30) γ-globulin, (31) Pepsin, (32) Lysozyme, (33) Human serum albumin. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA-Au NCs with different concentration of DOX and relationship between I
510
/I
640 and DOX concentration.
Comparison of different analytical methods for determination of DOX.

row.entry: UV–vis of citrate coated gold nanoparticles

entry.cross-ref: Mu, Liu, Xu, Tian, and Luan (2012)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of DOX in practical samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorometric probe for doxycycline in food by using bovine serum albumin protected Au nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agronomy, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Yinyin Chen and Xini Wang should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doxycycline (DOX) is widely used as feed additive and may remain in animal food. Thus, the detection of DOX in food field has become important. This work has reported a simple and rapid strategy to detect DOX by ratiometric fluorescence method without any sensitization materials based on bovine serum albumin (BSA) protected Au nanoclusters (BSA-Au NCs). Under excitation at 390 nm, the BSA-Au NCs has displayed two distinct emission peaks at 510 nm and 640 nm which are respectively originated from BSA and Au NCs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I 510/I 640 of BSA-Au NCs probe has presented a good linear range (0.1–30 μM) and 0.006 μM limit of detection for DOX. This method was used successfully to detect DOX in food samples and the good recoveries reached 96.7–103.7%. The research has showed that potential application of BSA-Au NCs in food field because of its convenient synthesis scheme and excellent selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline, an efficient and broad-spectrum semi-synthetic tetracycline antibiotic, is extensively used as a feed additive to prevent and treat animal infections. After excessive addition of antibiotics in the feed, the antibiotics will remain in animal tissues or by-products, and endanger human health through the food chain seriously, when they are not completely metabolized (Ashley, Feng, & Sun, 2018). Therefore, detection of DOX in food field is very essential and significant.

Several methods for detecting DOX in food have been reported so far, including high performance liquid chromatography (Hadad, El-Gindy, & Mahmoud, 2008), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Permana, Tekkoa, McCarthy, & Donnelly, 2019), enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (Adrian, Fernandez, Sanchez-Baeza, & Marco, 2012), capillary electrophoresis (Ibarra, Rodriguez, Miranda, Vega, & Barrado, 2011), electrochemical (Xu, Jiang, Liu, & Yang, 2020). Although these methods can provide sensitive and accurate multiplex analysis detection, these techniques are usually complex and time-consuming, and require expensive instruments and professionally trained personnel to operate. Consequently, these methods may limit on-site, rapid and real-time detection of DOX (Siddiqui et al., 2020). Due to the above problems, it is very important to develop a rapid, actionable, highly selective and sensitive analytical technique for qualitative and quantitative detection of DOX in food. The fluorescence sensing method has the advantages of high selectivity, prompt response, and wide dynamic linear range (C. Duan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019; Zeng, Wu, Hu, Yuan, & Bao, 2019). The ratiometic fluorescence takes the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at two different fluorescence peaks as the response signal. Compared with single emission fluorescent probes, the ratiometic fluorescence can eliminate the fluctuations in fluorescence intensity caused by certain factors through self-calibratio. Thus, the sample application range of ratio fluorescence method is wider than that of direct fluorescence spectroscopy, and it usually has higher sensitivity and better selectivity (Mudliar, Dongre, & Singh, 2020; Pandey & Singh, 2020; V. R.; Singh & Singh, 2020). At present, ratio fluorescent probes have been widely used in biological component detection (Mudliar, Dongre, & Singh, 2019, 2021; Vidya R.; Singh, Pandey, & Singh, 2021), environmental analysis (Desai & Singh, 2018; Liu et al., 2020), food detection (N. Duan et al., 2021; Q. Hu, Li, Qin, Zeng, & Hou, 2018; Zhu et al., 2021), medical diagnosis (Y. M. Yang, Zhao, Feng, & Li, 2013) and other fields.

In recent years, many new optical probes have been synthesized, including fluorescent proteins (Chudakov, Matz, Lukyanov, & Lukyanov, 2010), small molecules (H. Singh, Tiwari, Tiwari, Pramanik, & Das, 2019) and other nanoparticles. Gold nanoclusters have been widely used as analytical probes for detection of certain substance in biological and chemical systems due to their special physical and chemical properties. Zhang et al. has reported a functionalized gold nanocluster based on chelation-enhanced fluorescence and detect of phosphate in human serum (Z. P. Zhang, Feng, Huang, Li, & Wu, 2019). Zhao et al. detects trinitrotoluene by framework-enhanced fluorescence of gold nanoclusters (Y. Zhao et al., 2020). There are many methods for synthesizing gold nanoclusters. For instance, Song et al. has utilized D-His as the reducing and stabilizing agent to produce D-His@Au NCs. The catalytic activity of D-His@Au NCs greatly decreases via Cu2+-induced aggregation and is restored upon the addition of DOX, which can achieve the purpose of the detecting DOX (Y. Y. Song, Qiao, Liu, & Qi, 2020). Jain et al. has reported Au NCs with BSA as the reducing and stabilizing agent is used for the detection of hydrogen peroxide levels in liver cells by reactive oxygen species (ROS) quenching single emission peaks at 651 nm from BSA-Au NCs (Jain, Bhagat, & Singh, 2021). So far, Au NCs have been achieved the purpose of single-emitting and ratiometric fluorescence detection by embedding, assembling or conjugating other fluorescent nanomaterials. Intrinsic dual-emitting Au NCs in analytical sensing have seldom been reported, except that Yang et al. pioneeringly has found the gold nanoparticles synthesized with glutathione has intrinsic dual-emitting peaks at 610 nm and 810 nm respectively (Y. Yang et al., 2019). There has not been a study reported the BSA-Au NCs with intrinsic dual-emitting peaks as ratiometric fluorescent probes in analytical sensing.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and HAuCl4·4H2O were bought from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All antibiotics containing doxycycline (DOX) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. The hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, ethanol, metal salt reagents, amino acid were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Co., Ltd. Proteins were bought from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytical grade and all solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solutions with double distilled water. All the fluorescence spectrum were obtained from a F-4500 Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan).

Refer to a typical experiment (J. P. Xie, Zheng, & Ying, 2009), after cleaning equipment by aqua regia prepared with hydrochloric acid and nitric acid, 10 mL of aqueous HAuCl4·4H2O solution (10 mM) was added into the 10 mL of BSA solution (50 mg/mL). The process of stirring and mixing was under 37 °C. After 2 min, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 12 by using NaOH solution (1 M). This was stirred continuously at 37 °C for 12 h. With the extension of the stirring time, the color of the compound changes from light yellow to reddish brown, and the solution shows strong red fluorescence under ultraviolet light.

The 1 mM DOX solution was diluted with double distilled water so that the concentration of DOX was 0, 0.1, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20 and 30 μM, respectively. DOX solutions of different concentrations and BSA-Au NCs solution were mixed in equal volume to construct BSA-Au NCs/DOX determination system. In order to investigate system fluorescence enhancement effect of BSA-Au NCs/DOX system, fluorescence measurement was performed at 10 nm for excitation and emission slit widths, 390 nm for excitation wavelength. The excitation-emission spectra were record from 410 nm to 750 nm.

Pork, chicken liver, milk, eggs, and honey were selected as test samples. DOX was widely used as a material additive in above samples, which may cause residues in animal-derived foods. All samples were purchased from a local supermarket. Refer to the methods of Le et al. and Song et al. to pre-process the sample (Le, Zhao, Wei, & Bi, 2012; J. P.; Song et al., 2017). Firstly, 2 mL of milk or 1 g of other samples was put into a 10 mL beaker and diluted with 5 mL phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH = 12, 0.01 M). Then 1 mL of 5% trichloroacetic acid was add to the beaker. The supernatant of the mixture was obtained after vortexing for 10 min and centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Adjust the pH of the supernatant to 12 with 1 M NaOH and centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and dilute the supernatant for the further spiked-recovery analysis according to the determination procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of BSA-Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The particle sizes and morphologies of the prepared BSA-Au NCs were shown in Fig. 2 a. As displayed in Fig. 2a, BSA-Au NCs were roughly spherical and distributed apparent aggregation. Inset of Fig. 2a has represented the statistically analyzed of size distribution resulting of 100 nanoparticles. This has verified that the size distribution of the nanomaterial is narrow. According to size distribution, diameters of the particles are 0.5–3.5 nm with the average value is 2.06 nm. The surface structure was also investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The functional groups of BSA-Au NCs was verified by a FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2b, a broad absorption band at 3419 cm−1 and a band at 2955 cm−1 are due to primary amines and C–H vibration, respectively (Govindaraju, Ankireddy, Viswanath, Kim, & Yun, 2017). The characteristic stretching and bending vibrations of amide I and amide II of BSA are respectively identified at 1633 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1. The peak at 1633 cm−1 may due to CO groups stretch and the peak at 1568 cm−1 may attribute to C–N stretch coupled with N–H bending mode, which both are related to the secondary structure of BSA (Tian, Liu, Hu, & Chen, 2005). The bands at 657 cm−1 may stem from –NH2 and –NH wagging (Retnakumari et al., 2011). This phenomenon has verified the BSA coating on Au NCs successfully. XPS for surface analysis in Fig. 2c illustrates binding energies of C, N, O, Au and Na, in BSA-Au NCs. The elemental analysis results are determined to be as follows: C 64.96%, N 14.55%, O 18.60% and Au 0.23% and Na 1.66%. Elements containing C, N, and O originat from BSA, while the Au element comes from Au NCs, and the Na derives from NaOH. From Fig. 2c, the appearance of representative signals of C1s at around 285 eV, N1s at around 400 eV, O1s at around 532 eV and Au4f at around 84 eV has been obviously observed on BSA-Au NCs surface. The binding energies of C1at 284.8, 285.9, and 287.9 eV correspond to C–C, C–O/C–N and CO, respectively. The N 1s peaks of 399.9 eV may be attributed to C–N and the O 1s peaks of 531.5 eV may be caused by C–N (Chandirasekar, You, Xue, & Tseng, 2019; Han et al., 2018; Jeong et al., 2014; Niu, Zhao, Sun, Shi, & Gong, 2016; Yu et al., 2008; Y. H.; Zhang, Nie, et al., 2019). This also proves that the BSA layer has been coated onto the surface of BSA-Au NCs.

In Fig. 2d, the binding energies of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 are 83.9(Au0) and 87.65 eV(Au3+), respectively (M. Zhang et al., 2013), respectively. The above proves the successful synthesis of BSA-Au NCs, in which most of the gold atoms are close to the Au0. This happening process can be speculated as follows. Initially, at neutral pH, owing to the interaction between protein and Au3+, the gold atoms from HAuCl4 were reduced from Au3+ to Au+, when BSA was mixed with HAuCl4 solution. Then, BSA-Au NCs were formed after adjusting the pH of the solution by NaOH. Finally, most of the Au3+ were reduced to Au0 when the pH was increased to 12 (Chaudhari, Xavier, & Pradeep, 2011). There were 21 tyrosine (Tyr) residues and 35 cysteine (Cys) residues in a BSA monomer. Tyr residues can reduce Au3+ through their phenolic groups by adjusting the pH. With the addition of NaOH, disulfide bonds of Cys were broken down into thiol functional groups. Thus, the sulfhydryl functional group (-SH) of Cys can be used as a stabilizing agent for BSA-Au NCs (J. P. Xie et al., 2009). This shows that alkaline conditions can activate the reducing ability of BSA molecules and BSA can be used as a reducing agent to reduce HAuCl4.

BSA-Au NCs solution with uniform and transparent appearance is reddish brown under sunlight but exhibits red fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 3 a inset). The above result indicates that synthesized BSA-Au NCs have high water solubility and photoluminescence. As shown in Fig. 3a, the black curve represents the UV–vis spectrum. There is a charactermatic protein absorbance peak at 275 nm, mainly from the aromatic and heterocyclic residues of BSA (X. L. Hu, Wu, Fang, Li, & Wang, 2016). There is no localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption band at 520 nm in the UV–vis spectrum. This appearance corresponded with absorption characteristics of Au NCs less than 3 nm, which is consistent with HRTEM results (Zhou, Shi, Chen, Wang, & Chen, 2009). In addition, blue curve is excitation spectrum and the red curve is the corresponding emission spectrum under above excitation. When the excitation wavelength is 400 nm, the BSA-Au NCs show intrinsic dual-emitting wavelengths at 480 nm and 640 nm, which is consistent with photoluminescence spectra from the trypsin-stabilized Au25 NCs. Therefore, it is speculated that BSA-Au NCs consist of 25 gold atoms (Au25) (Kawasaki, Yoshimura, Hamaguchi, & Arakawa, 2011). The peak at 480 nm may be due to the faint blue fluorescence from BSA and the peak at 640 nm can be contributed from six –S–Au(I)–S– Au(I)–S− staples (Wen, Yu, Toh, & Tang, 2012). The performance of optical properties can prove that BSA-Au NCs has the potential as ratio fluorescent probe.

BSA-Au NCs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 3b). As the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 310 nm to 410 nm, the left peak of emission wavelength has red-shift from 456 nm to 495 nm. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity has been strengthened. The magnitude of the redshifts is only 39 nm, which means that a simple energy transfer has occurred in the BSA-Au NCs (C. F. Lu et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the position of the right emission peak at 640 nm is fixed during excitation variation and fluorescence intensity gradually increases to the maximum under excitation at 390 nm. Thus, 390 nm is chosen as the excitation wavelength in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the BSA-Au NCs for DOX
ce:section.ce:para: The yellow fluorescence of DOX under alkaline conditions ( Fig. 5 a) may be due to the carbonyl ketone at C12 of the B ring occurred inter-molecular interconversion. On the other hand, the hydroxyl at C5 may become an exogenous negative ion to attack C12 with intramolecular nucleophilic. In this case, the structure of the B ring changes after the electron transfer, resulting in an isomer with lactonic structure (Yibing Zhao, Ci, & Chang, 1997). From Fig. 5b, under alkaline conditions, there is an absorption peak at 375 nm in the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of BSA-Au NCs + DOX and BSA + DOX. The position of this peak overlaps with the position of the absorption peak of alkaline DOX, which indicates that the fluorescence generated at 510 nm comes from alkaline DOX. In the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5c), both BSA + DOX (pH=12) and BSA-Au NCs + DOX (pH=12) produced green fluorescence peaks, indicating that the interaction between BSA-Au NCs and DOX is mainly based on the interaction between BSA and DOX. When DOX is added to BSA in alkaline solution, the fluorescence intensity increases, which is attributed to the sensitization effect of BSA on DOX (Mukherjee et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2017). BSA has a hydrophobic cavity and two Trp residues (Lou, Zhou, Pan, Shen, & Shi, 2017). Trp-134 residues are located on the surface of subdomain IB, while Trp-212 residues are located in the hydrophobic cavity of subdomain IIA (Burgos et al., 2011; Chatterjee & Mukherjee, 2014). DOX can enter the hydrophobic cavity of BSA and bind to the binding site of BSA (Khan, Muzammil, & Musarrat, 2002). When pH = 6.65 changes to pH = 12, the emission peak center of BSA is red-shifted from 468 nm to 473 nm (Fig. 5c), which indicates that the change of pH increases the polarity around Trp residues and the content of α-helix decreases (Cao, Li, Yue, & Wu, 2013; L. Y.; Xie, Wehling, Ciftci, & Zhang, 2017). Therefore, the structure of BSA protein is expand and energy transfer level of aromatic amino acids between residues are reduced. This promotes the binding between DOX and hydrophobic sites of BSA through non-covalent bonds, further causing fluorescence enhancement (Chi et al., 2018; Popov, Vaptzarova, Kossekova, & Nikolov, 1972).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive determination of DOX
ce:section.ce:para:
Excellent selectivity is an essential characteristic for an implementable detection system. Thus, we investigate selectivity of BSA-Au NCs to DOX under identical conditions. The concentrations of DOX and other target molecules were 15 μM. As shown in Fig. 6 a, compared with other target molecules, such as oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), carbenicillin sodium (CB), amikacin sulfate (AMK), azithromycin (AZM), pefloxacin mesylate (PEF), tilmicosin phosphate (TIL), marbofloxacin (MBF), apramycin sulfate (APR), enrofloxacin hydrochloride (ENR), streptomycin sulfate (STR), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and the metal ions which includes Ca2+, K+,Mg2+, Fe3+, BSA-Au NCs has outstanding selectivity and specificity for DOX detection. Furthermore, in Fig. 6b, the interference of BSA-Au NCs on DOX is established and BSA-Au NCs have little effect on the detection of DOX in the presence of other antibiotics, metal ions, amino acids and proteins. The inset of Fig. 6c presents the color changes of the solution under a 365 nm UV lamp upon DOX addition. The color has changed from red to yellow as the concentration of DOX increased (0–30 μM). Meanwhile, I
510 enhances gradually but I
640 is unchanged when increases concentration of DOX. Taking I
640 as a reference, the ratio of I
510
/I
640 has a good linear relationship with the range of DOX (0.1–30 μM) (Fig. 6c). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.006 μM, based on 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and 's' was the slope of Fig. 6c. This result is comparable or lower than other detection methods, and the detection range is relatively wide (Table 1 ). The above results have declared that BSA-Au NCs can achieve high-efficiency ratiometric detection of DOX. Without additional labelled dye and complicated complex, the easy and environmentally friendly preparation of BSA-Au NCs is superior to currently reported detection materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in food sample
ce:section.ce:para: The developed assay has been used to determine DOX in pork, chicken liver, milk, eggs and honey (Table 2 ). Spiked different concentrations of DOX into the practical samples without DOX, the average recoveries are assessed. In the case, the recovery rate is well (96.7–104%) and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is no more than 5% (Table 2), which meets the standards for actual quantitative measurement. This shows this method can successfully detect DOX in food field.

In summary, this work has developed a simple and rapid strategy to detect DOX in food samples by ratiometric fluorescence method without any sensitization materials. The detection strategy is based on the unique interaction between BSA protected Au nanoclusters and DOX. This method is low-cost and d their extraordinary selectivity and sensitivity, and has a wider range from 0.1 μM to 30 μM and lower LOD is 0.006 μM for DOX. In addition, it was used successfully for fluorescence detection DOX in foods, which indicated that it had potential prospects to be applied in food safety application.

Yinyin Chen: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xini Wang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Changfang Lu: Formal analysis. Wei Wu: Formal analysis. Xianxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305097) and the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 03572393 and No. 1921993220).
